original_abstract:
Three-dimensional (3D) organic–inorganic lead halide perovskites have rapidly become frontrunners in the development of low-cost, high-efficiency optoelectronic devices. This surge in interest has highlighted the potential of specialized subclasses, such as two-dimensional (2D) halide perovskites, which are now fundamental to our understanding of the chemical and physical properties necessary for next-generation technology. While 2D and 3D perovskites share similar chemistries, the layered structure of 2D materials—characterized by a hybrid organic–inorganic interface—gives rise to emergent properties that can dramatically alter material behavior. 

By combining these different dimensionalities, researchers can exploit their intrinsic compatibility to create synergistic systems. These mixed-dimensional heteroarchitectures often mitigate the individual weaknesses of each material; for example, 3D–2D perovskites can achieve levels of stability and performance that neither could reach alone. This review explores how structural differences between 3D and 2D phases result in disparate material properties and details solution-processing strategies for engineering mixed-dimensional architectures, ranging from interfacial "capping" to integrated composites. We provide a comprehensive outlook on the role of 3D–2D systems in advancing solar cell efficiency and durability, conclude by examining applications beyond photovoltaics, and offer a perspective on these systems as a semiconductor class with unrivaled tunability.
humanized_abstract:
Three-dimensional (3D) organic–inorganic lead halide perovskites have become a focus of intense research in recent years due to their potential for producing low-cost, efficient optoelectronic devices. A number of subclasses of these materials, including 2D halide perovskites, are now known in some detail and are beginning to provide insight into the requirements for materials that will be useful for the realization of new optoelectronic concepts. The 2D and 3D materials share a common chemistry, but the 2D materials consist of a layered structure with a hybrid organic–inorganic interface, which gives rise to unusual physical properties.

By integrating these three types of dimensionality in a mutually compatible fashion, it is possible to build hybrid systems with truly synergetic behavior. Mixed-dimensional heteroarchitectures can compensate the weaknesses of the individual components in such a way that, e.g., 3D–2D perovskites can have superior stability and performance. The starting point here is that the 3D and 2D structural features lead to quite different physical properties. Some basic solution-processing techniques to prepare 3D–2D hybrids are also discussed. This ranges from simple capping methods to the preparation of composites where all features are strongly integrated with each other. This review provides an overview of the potential of 3D–2D perovskites to solve the solar cell efficiency and stability challenge, and provides an outlook towards other applications of such hybrid semiconductor materials. Finally, the potential of 3D–2D hybrid perovskites as a semiconductor class with the highest possible tunability is discussed.
